architecture.summary: Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding (DCCD) is a two-stage, training-free inference procedure that decouples semantic planning from structural enforcement. Stage 1 generates an unconstrained draft (free-form reasoning or semantic plan) from a draft model. Stage 2 applies constrained decoding (token masking and renormalization via a constraint oracle) conditioned on the draft context, using a projector model (which may be the same as the draft model or smaller). The draft is concatenated into the context before the constrained decoding step, increasing feasible mass (probability assigned to valid tokens) before hard constraints are enforced. The final output is guaranteed to satisfy the structural constraint while the draft conditioning reduces constraint-induced distortion.

biblio.title: The Hidden Cost of Structured Generation in LLMs: Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding

claims.statement: DCCD improves strict structured accuracy by up to +24 percentage points over standard constrained decoding
On GSM8K with a 1B model, DCCD improves strict structured accuracy from 15.24% to 39.04%
On GSM8K with a 1.5B model, DCCD improves from 49.36% to 73.92%
On MATH500, a DCCD composition (3B total) achieves 12.7 accuracy per billion parameters, compared to 3.6 for an 8B model using constrained decoding, a 253% efficiency improvement
On GSM8K, DCCD improves from 78% at n=1 to 83% at n=13, while constrained decoding improves from 64% to 73%
Decode-time constrained decoding costs 8–20 percentage points in answer accuracy across six model sizes (1B–14B), with the largest drops on smaller models (e.g., 15 points for 1.5B model)

Constrained decoding induces a cumulative reverse-KL projection tax across token positions, with per-step distortion KL(p_base || p_constrained) = -log(feasible_mass), and sequence-level distortion summing to KL(π_base || π_CD) = Σ_t -log(m_t(x₁:t-₁))
DCCD enables parameter-efficient model composition where smaller model pairs match or exceed much larger constrained baselines
